content: 4 point charges each +q is placed on the circumference of a circle of diameter 2d in such a way that they form a square. The potential at the centre is
explanation:
Potential at centre due to all charges are

$$ = {1 \over {4\pi { \varepsilon _0}}}\left[ {{q \over d} + {q \over d} + {q \over d} + {q \over d}} \right]$$

$$ = {1 \over {4\pi { \varepsilon _0}}}{{4q} \over d}$$ in S.I. units

$$ = {{4q} \over d}$$ in C.G.S. units

content: 64 identical spheres of charge q and capacitance C each are combined to form a large sphere. The charge and capacitance of the large sphere is
explanation:
64 drops have formed a single drop of radius R.

$$\therefore$$ $${4 \over 3}\pi {R^3} = 64{4 \over 3}\pi {r^3} \Rightarrow R = 4r$$

$${Q_{total}} = 64q$$

As $$C' = {Q \over V}$$ and $$V = {1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}{Q \over R}$$

$$C' = 4\pi {\varepsilon _0}R$$

$$C' = (4\pi {\varepsilon _0})\,.\,4r \Rightarrow C' = 4C$$.

content: Consider a particle of mass 1 gm and charge 1.0 Coulomb is at rest. Now the particle is subjected to an electric field $E(t)=E_0 \sin \omega t$ in the $x$-direction, where $E_0=2$ Newton/Coulomb and $\omega=1000 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}$. The maximum speed attained by the particle is
explanation:
The equation of motion is

$$m\ddot x=qE_0\sin\omega t\,,\quad m=0.001\rm\;kg,\;q=1\;C,\;E_0=2\;N/C,\;\omega=1000\;s^{-1}\,. $$

Integrate once (with $v(0)=0$) to get

$$v(t)=\int_0^t\frac{qE_0}{m}\sin(\omega t')\,dt' =\frac{qE_0}{m\omega}\bigl[1-\cos(\omega t)\bigr] =\frac{2}{0.001\cdot1000}\bigl(1-\cos\omega t\bigr) =2\bigl(1-\cos\omega t\bigr)\,(\rm m/s).$$

Since $\cos\omega t$ ranges from $-1$ to $1$, the maximum speed is

$$v_{\max}=2\bigl(1-(-1)\bigr)=4\;\rm m/s.$$

Answer: 4 m/s.

content: Two charges $+q$ and $-q$ are placed at points $A$ and $B$ respectively which are at a distance $2 L_{\mathrm{p} p a t}$ $C$ is the mid point of $A$ and $B$. The workdone in moving a charge $+Q$ along the semicircle $\operatorname{CSD}\left(W_V\right)$ and along the line $\mathrm{CBD}\left(W_2\right)$ are
explanation:
Since, electrostatic force is conservative in nature, we use work energy theorem,

$$ \begin{aligned} & W_{C S D}=\text { change in potential energy }, \\ & W_{C S D}=Q\left(V_D-V_C\right) \,\,\,\,\,\,\left[\because V_C=0\right]\\ & W_{C S D}=Q\left[\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \cdot 3 L}-\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \cdot L}\right] \\ & W_{C S D}=\frac{Q q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 L}\left[\frac{1}{3}-1\right]=\frac{-2 Q q}{12 \pi \varepsilon_0 L} \\ & W_{C S D}=-\frac{Q q}{6 \pi \varepsilon_0 L} \end{aligned} $$

And also, $\quad W_{C S D}=W_{C B D}$

                           $W_1=W_2$

(Independent of path)

content: A charge Q is placed at the centre of a cube of sides a. The total flux of electric field through the six surfaces of the cube is
explanation:
To find the total flux of the electric field through the six surfaces of the cube, we can use Gauss's Law. Gauss's Law states that the total electric flux through a closed surface is proportional to the charge enclosed within that surface. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$ \Phi_E = \frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{\epsilon_0} $$

where:

  • $$\Phi_E$$ is the electric flux
  • $$Q_{\text{enc}}$$ is the charge enclosed within the surface
  • $$\epsilon_0$$ is the permittivity of free space

In this scenario, the charge $$Q$$ is placed at the center of the cube. The cube is a closed surface, and according to Gauss's Law, the total flux through this surface only depends on the enclosed charge $$Q$$ and not on the dimensions of the cube.

Therefore, the total flux $$\Phi_E$$ through the six surfaces of the cube is:

$$ \Phi_E = \frac{Q}{\epsilon_0} $$

So, the correct answer is:

Option C

$$ \mathrm{Q} / \epsilon_0 $$

content: Three point charges $$\mathrm{q},-2 \mathrm{q}$$ and $$\mathrm{q}$$ are placed along $$x$$ axis at $$x=-{a}, 0$$ and $a$ respectively. As $$\mathrm{a} \rightarrow 0$$ and $$\mathrm{q} \rightarrow \infty$$ while $$\mathrm{q} \mathrm{a}^2=\mathrm{Q}$$ remains finite, the electric field at a point P, at a distance $$x(x \gg a)$$ from $$x=0$$ is $$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{\alpha \mathrm{Q}}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 x^\beta} \hat{i}$$. Then
explanation:
Given, $$q{a^2} = Q$$

and $$x > > a \Rightarrow {q \over x} < < 1$$

Here we will apply the concept of Binomial approximation.

net electric field at point P,

$${E_P} = {{kq} \over {{{(x + a)}^2}}} + {{kq} \over {{{(x - a)}^2}}} - {{2kq} \over {{x^2}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {E_P} = {{kq} \over {{x^2}}}\left[ {{1 \over {{{\left( {1 + {q \over x}} \right)}^2}}} + {1 \over {{{\left( {1 - {q \over x}} \right)}^2}}} - 2} \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow {E_P} = {{kq} \over {{x^2}}}\left[ {{{\left( {1 + {q \over x}} \right)}^{ - 2}} + {{\left( {1 - {q \over x}} \right)}^{ - 2}} - 2} \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow {E_P} = {{kq} \over {{x^2}}}\left[ {1 - {{2q} \over x} + {1 \over 2}( - 2)( - 3){{\left( {{q \over x}} \right)}^2} + 1 + {{2q} \over x} + {1 \over 2}( - 2)( - 3){{\left( {{q \over x}} \right)}^2} - 2} \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow {E_P} = {{kq} \over {{x^2}}}\left[ {6{{{q^2}} \over {{x^2}}}} \right]$$

$$ = {{6k(q{a^2})} \over {{x^4}}} = {{6kQ} \over {{x^4}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {E_P} = {{6Q} \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{x^4}}}\widehat i$$

$$ = {{\alpha Q} \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{x^\beta }}}\widehat i$$ (given)

Hence, $$\alpha=6,\beta=4$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\alpha \over \beta } = {6 \over 4} = {3 \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \alpha = {3 \over 2}\beta $$

content: Consider a positively charged infinite cylinder with uniform volume charge density $$\rho > 0$$. An electric dipole consisting of + Q and $$-$$ Q charges attached to opposite ends of a massless rod is oriented as shown in the figure. At the instant as shown in the figure, the dipole will experience,

options.content: a force to the left and no torque.
a force to the right and a clockwise torque.
a force to the right and a counter clockwise torque.
no force but only a clockwise torque.

explanation:
The top view of the cylinder and the dipole is

Hence, the dipole will experience a net force and a net torque in clockwise direction.

content: A thin glass rod is bent in a semicircle of radius R. A charge is non-uniformly distributed along the rod with a linear charge density $$\lambda=\lambda_0\sin\theta$$ ($$\lambda_0$$ is a positive constant). The electric field at the centre P of the semicircle is,
explanation: "W" means the question is withdrawn. Hence, marks will be awarded to all appeared candidates.

content: The figure represents two equipotential lines in x-y plane for an electric field. The x-component E$$_x$$ of the electric field in space between these equipotential lines is,
explanation:
The x-component of E is given by

$$E_x=-\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta x}=-\frac{2-4}{2-4}=-1 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{cm}=-100 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$$

content: An electric dipole of dipole moment $$\vec{p}$$ is placed at the origin of the co-ordinate system along the $$\mathrm{z}$$-axis. The amount of work required to move a charge '$$\mathrm{q}$$' from the point $$(\mathrm{a}, 0, 0)$$ to the point $$(0,0, a)$$ is,
explanation:
The work required to move a charge $$(q)$$ from point $$A$$ to $$B$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} W_{\mathrm{ext}} & =q\left(V_B-V_A\right)=q\left(\frac{k p}{a^2}-0\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{P q}{a^2} \quad\left(\because k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\right) \end{aligned}$$

content: The electric potential for an electric field directed parallel to X-axis is shown in the figure. Choose the correct plot of electric field strength.
explanation:
We know that, $$E = - {{dV} \over {dr}}$$

Thus, electric field from ($$-$$4 to $$-$$2),

$$E = - \left( {{{10 - 0} \over {4 - ( - 2)}}} \right) = {{10} \over 2} = 5$$ N/C

Electric field from ($$-$$2 to 2),

$$E = - \left( {{{10 - 10} \over {2 - ( - 2)}}} \right) = 0$$

Electric field from (2 to 7),

$$E = - \left( {{{10 - 0} \over {2 - 7}}} \right) = - 2$$ N/C

content: Two charges, each equal to $$-$$q are kept at ($$-$$a, 0) and (a, 0). A charge q is placed at the origin. If q is given a small displacement along y direction, the force acting on q is proportional to
explanation:
Consider the diagram given below.

When the charge q is displaced momentarily, it starts oscillating. The net force acting on q is given by $${F_{net}} = 2F\sin \theta $$

$$ = - {{2\,kq \times q} \over {{{(\sqrt {{y^2} + {a^2}} )}^2}}}\,.\,{y \over {\sqrt {{y^2} + {a^2}} }} = - {{2k{q^2}y} \over {{{(\sqrt {{y^2} + {a^2}} )}^{{3 \over 2}}}}}$$

For $$a > > y$$, we have

$${F_{net}} = - {{2k{q^2}y} \over {{a^3}}}$$

or, $${F_{net}} \propto - y$$

content: A neutral conducting solid sphere of radius R has two spherical cavities of radius a and b as shown in the figure. Centre to centre distance between two cavities is c. qa and qb charges are placed at the centres of cavities respectively. The force between qa and qb is
explanation:
We can find the force between $${q_a}$$ and $${q_b}$$ by calculating electric field first.

From Gauss's law,

$$\oint {E\,.\,ds = {{{Q_{enclosed}}} \over {{\varepsilon _0}}} \Rightarrow E\,.\,4\pi {c^2} = {{{q_a}} \over {{\varepsilon _0}}}} $$

($$\because$$ For cavity of electric field is to be calculated upto $${q_b}$$ )

$$ \Rightarrow {E_a} = {{{q_a}} \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{c^2}}}$$

Now, force on $${q_b}$$ due to $${q_a}$$ is given by

$$F = {E_a}\,.\,{q_b} = {{{q_a}{q_b}} \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{c^2}}}$$

content: Consider two concentric conducting sphere of radii R and 2R respectively. The inner sphere is given a charge +Q. The other sphere is grounded. The potential at $$r = {{3R} \over 2}$$ is
explanation:
Consider the figure given below.

Due to grounding, charge on outer sphere = 0

Now, potential due to inner sphere,

$$V = {{kQ} \over r} = {{kQ} \over {{{3R} \over 2}}}$$

$$ = {{2kQ} \over {3R}}$$

or, $${1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}{{2Q} \over {3R}}$$

content: The variation of electric field along the Z-axis due to a uniformly charged circular ring of radius a in XY-plane as shown in the figure. The value of coordinate M will be
explanation:
The electric field along Z-axis due to uniformly charged circular ring of radius a in XY-plane is given by

$$E = {{kqz} \over {{{({z^2} + {a^2})}^{3/2}}}}$$

where, q is the net charge on the ring and z is axial distance on Z-axis from the centre of the ring. Now, at point M in the graph shown, electric field is maximum.

So,

$${{dE} \over {dZ}} = {{kq{{({z^2} + {a^2})}^{3/2}} - {3 \over 2}{{({z^2} + {a^2})}^{3/2}}.\,2{z^2}} \over {({z^2} + {a^2})}}$$

For maxima, $${{dE} \over {dZ}} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {({z^2} + {a^2})^{3/2}} = {3 \over 2} \times 2{z^2}{({z^2} + {a^2})^{1/2}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {z^2} + {a^2} = 3{z^2} \Rightarrow 2{z^2} = {a^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {z \over a} = {1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}$$

content: A metal sphere of radius R carrying charge q is surrounded by a thick concentric metal shell of inner and outer radii a and b, respectively. The net charge on the shell is zero. The potential at the centre of the sphere, when the outer surface of the shell is grounded will be
explanation:
The given situation is shown below


A radial electric field E exists in the region between the two shells due to charge on inner shell only.

Electric field at point P is calculated according to Gauss's law,

i.e. $${\phi _E} = E\,.\,4\pi {r^2} = {q \over {{\varepsilon _0}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow E = {q \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}}$$ .... (i)

The potential at the centre of sphere,

$$V = - \int_a^b {E\,.\,dr = \int_a^b {E\,.\,dr = \int_a^b {{q \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}}dr} } } $$ [from Eq. (i)]

$$ \Rightarrow V = {q \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\left( {{1 \over a} - {1 \over b}} \right)$$

content: Three infinite plane sheets carrying uniform charge densities $$-$$ $$\sigma$$, 2$$\sigma$$, 4$$\sigma$$ are placed parallel to XZ-plane at Y = a, 3a, 4a respectively. The electric field at the point (0, 2a, 0) is
explanation:
The given situation is shown in the figure


We have to calculate electric field at point P (0, 2a, 0).

Net electric field at point P,

$$E = {{ - \sigma } \over {2{\varepsilon _0}}}\widehat j - {{2\sigma } \over {2{\varepsilon _0}}}\widehat j - {{4\sigma } \over {2{\varepsilon _0}}}\widehat j$$

$$E = {{ - 7\sigma } \over {2{\varepsilon _0}}}\widehat j$$

content: Two point charges +q<sub>1</sub> and +q<sub>2</sub> are placed a finite distance d apart. It is desired to put a third charge q<sub>3</sub> in between these two charges, so that q<sub>3</sub> is in equilibrium. This is

options.content: possible only if q<sub>3</sub> is negative.
possible only if q<sub>3</sub> is positive.
possible irrespective of the sign of q<sub>3</sub>.

explanation:
The given situation is shown below


Case 1 If charge q3 is taken as positives.

In this situation, force on charge q3 due to charges q1 and q2 will be repulsive and in opposite direction. Hence, at a certain location between +q1 and +q2, charge q3 will be in equilibrium, when both repulsive forces will be equal in magnitude.

Case 2 If charge q3 is taken as negative.

In this situation, force on charge q3 due to charges q1 and q2 will be attractive and in opposite direction. Hence, at a certain location between +q1 and +q2, charge q3 will be in equilibrium, when both attractive forces will be equal in magnitude.

content:
As shown in the figure, a point charge q1 = + 1 $$ \times $$ 10-6 C is placed at the origin in xy-plane and another point charge q2 = + 3 $$ \times $$ 10-6 C is placed at the coordinate (10, 0).


In that case, which of the following graph(s) shows most correctly the electric field vector in Ex in x-direction?
explanation:
Given, q1 = 1 $$ \times $$ 10-6 C, q2 = 3 $$ \times $$ 10-6 C

$$ \therefore $$ q2 = 3q1 ; q2 > q1

As we know that, electric field, $$E\alpha {1 \over {{x^2}}}$$

So, the graph showing variation of Ex in x-direction is as given below.

content: Four identical point masses, each of mass m and carrying charge + q are placed at the corners of a square of sides a on a frictionless plane surface. If the particles are released simultaneously, the kinetic energy of the system when they are infinitely far apart is
explanation:
The given system of charges is as shown below,


$$ \because $$ Potential energy, $$PE = {1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}{{{q_1}{q_2}} \over r} = {{k{q_1}{q_2}} \over r}$$

Here, q1 = q2 = q

and r = a

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$PE = {{kqq} \over a} = {{k{q^2}} \over a}$$

Due to four identical point masses,

$$P{E_i} = {{4k{q^2}} \over a} + {{2k{q^2}} \over {\sqrt 2 a}}$$

According to law of conservation of energy,

KEi + PEi = KEf + PEf

Initially, KEi = 0

and at infinity, PEf = 0

$$ \therefore $$ $$K{E_f} = P{E_i} = {{4k{q^2}} \over a} + {{2k{q^2}} \over {\sqrt 2 a}}$$

= $${{k{q^2}} \over a}(4 + \sqrt 2 )$$

$$ \because $$In CGS unit, k = 1

$$ \therefore $$ KE = $${{{q^2}} \over a}(4 + \sqrt 2 )$$

content: A very long charged solid cylinder of radius a contains a uniform charge density p. Dielectric constant of the material of the cylinder is K. What will be the magnitude of electric field at a radial distance x (x < a) from the axis of the cylinder?
explanation:
Given, radius of solid cylinder = a

Charge density of cylinder = p

Dielectric constant of material of cylinder = k

The electric field at a radial distance x(x < a) as shown in calculated as


From Gauss's law,

$$\int {E.dA = {{{Q_{in}}} \over {k{\varepsilon _0}}}} $$ , where k is dielectric constant of material.

$$ \Rightarrow $$ E(2$$\pi $$xl) = $${{p(\pi {x^2}l)} \over {k{\varepsilon _0}}}$$ .................[$$ \because $$ charge, Qin = p($$\pi $$x2l)]

$$ \Rightarrow $$ E = $${{px} \over {2k{\varepsilon _0}}}$$

content: A pair of parallel metal plates are kept with a separation d. One plate is at a potential + V and the other is at ground potential. A narrow beam of electrons enters the space between the plates with a velocity v<sub>0</sub> and in a direction parallel to the plates. What will be the angle of the beam with the plates after it travels an axial distance L?
explanation:
Distance between the parallel plates = d

The axial distance of beam from the centre of parallel plates = L

As, time, t = $${{dis\tan ce} \over {velocity}} = {L \over {{v_0}}}$$


y-component of velocity, $${v_y} = \left( {{{qE} \over m}} \right)t = {e \over m}.{V \over d} \times {L \over {{v_0}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${v_y} = {{eVL} \over {md{v_0}}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$\tan \theta = {{{v_y}} \over {{v_z}}} = {{eLV} \over {md{v_0}.{v_0}}} = {{eVL} \over {mdv_0^2}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$\theta $$ = $${\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {{{eVL} \over {mdv_0^2}}} \right)$$

content: Two pith balls, each carrying charge + q are hung from a hook by two springs. It is found that when each charge is tripled, angle between the strings double. What was the initial angle between the strings?
explanation:
Let force acting due to electrostatic attraction of charges be Fe as shown in the figure below.


Now,
Tsin$$\theta $$ = Fe ................(i)

Tcos$$\theta $$ = mg ................(ii)

On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get

$${{T\sin \theta } \over {T\cos \theta }} = {{{F_e}} \over {mg}} = k{{{q_1} \times {q_2}} \over {{r^2}}}$$

Here, q1 = q2 = q

Distance, r = 2L sin$$\theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ tan$$\theta $$ = $${{k{q^2}} \over {{{(2L\sin \theta )}^2}.mg}}$$ ...........(iii)

Now, in the second case, when the charges are tripled, new charge q' = 3q

As the angle between the strings are doubled, $$\theta $$ becomes 2$$\theta $$, hence from Eq. (iii), we get

$$\tan 2\theta = {{k(3q)(3q)} \over {{{(2L\sin 2\theta )}^2}.mg}}$$

$$\tan 2\theta = {{9k{q^2}} \over {{{(2L\sin 2\theta )}^2}.mg}}$$ ..........(iv)

On dividing Eq. (iv) by Eq. (iii), we get

$${{\tan 2\theta } \over {\tan \theta }} = {{9k{q^2}} \over {4{L^2}{{\sin }^2}2\theta .mg}} \times {{4{L^2}{{\sin }^2}\theta .mg} \over {k{q^2}}}$$

$${{\tan 2\theta } \over {\tan \theta }} = {{9{{\sin }^2}\theta } \over {{{\sin }^2}2\theta }}$$

As, $$\tan 2\theta = {{2\tan \theta } \over {1 + {{\tan }^2}\theta }}$$

and $$\sin 2\theta $$ = 2 $$\sin \theta $$ $$\cos \theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${2 \over {1 - {{\tan }^2}\theta }} = {{9{{\sin }^2}\theta } \over {4{{\sin }^2}\theta {{\cos }^2}\theta }} = {9 \over {4{{\cos }^2}\theta }}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${2 \over {1 - {{\tan }^2}\theta }} = {9 \over 4}{\sec ^2}\theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${2 \over {1 - {{\tan }^2}\theta }} = {9 \over 4}(1 + {\tan ^2}\theta )$$

............[$$ \because $$ 1 + tan2$$\theta $$ = sec2$$\theta $$]

Let tan2$$\theta $$ = x

$${2 \over {1 - x}} = {9 \over 4}(1 + x)$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 8 = 9 $$ - $$ 9x2

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 9x2 = 1

$$ \Rightarrow $$ x2 = $${1 \over 9}$$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ x = $${1 \over 3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ tan2$$\theta $$ = $${1 \over 3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ tan$$\theta $$ = $${1 \over {\sqrt 3 }} = \tan 30^\circ $$

$$\theta $$ = 30$$^\circ $$

Hence, the angle between the strings

= 2$$\theta $$ = 2 $$ \times $$ 30$$^\circ $$ = 60$$^\circ $$

content: Eleven equal point charges, all of them having a charge +Q, are placed at all the hour positions of a circular clock of radius r, except at the 10 h position. What is the electric field strength at the centre of the clock?

options.content: $${Q \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}}$$ from the centre towards the mark 10
$${Q \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}}$$ from the mark 10 towards the centre
$${Q \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}}$$ from the centre towards the mark 6

explanation:
The clock diagram is as shown below


In the above diagram, charge +Q is not placed at 10 h position.

So, net electric field strength at centre C,

$${E_{net}} = {E_1} + {E_2} + {E_3} + {E_4} + {E_5} + {E_6} + {E_7} + {E_8} + {E_9} + {E_{11}} + {E_{12}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {E_{net}} = {E_1} + {E_2} + {E_3} + {E_4} + {E_5} + {E_6} + ( - {E_1} - {E_2} - {E_3} - {E_5} - {E_6})$$

$$ \Rightarrow {E_{net}} = {E_4} = {Q \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}}$$ from centre towards the mark 10

content: A negative charge is placed at the midpoint between two fixed equal positive charges, separated by a distance 2d. If the negative charge is given a small displacement x(x < < d) perpendicular to the line joining the positive charges, how the force (F) developed on it will approximately depend on x?

A point charge $$-$$ q is carried from a point A to another point B on the axis of a charged ring of radius r carrying a charge + q. If the point A is at a distance $${4 \over 3}r$$ from the centre of the ring and the point B is $${3 \over 4}r$$ from the centre but on the opposite side, what is the net work that need to be done for this?
explanation:
A charged circular ring of radius R is shown in figure.


From the figure, $$d = \sqrt {{x^2} + {r^2}} $$ ....(i)

$$ \therefore $$ Potential due to a uniform ring of positive charge q at point P,

$$V = {1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\,.\,{q \over d}$$

$$ = {1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\,.\,{q \over {\sqrt {{x^2} + {r^2}} }}$$ [from Eq. (i)]

Now,


$$ \therefore $$ $$CB = \sqrt {O{C^2} + O{B^2}} = \sqrt {{r^2} + {{\left( {{3 \over 4}} \right)}^2}{r^2}} = {5 \over 4}r$$

Similarly, $$AC = {5 \over 3}r$$

$$ \therefore $$ Work done in bringing a point charge $$-$$ q from A to B,

$$W = - q({V_B} - {V_A})$$

$$ = - q\left[ {{1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\,.\,{q \over {CB}} - {1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\,.\,{q \over {CA}}} \right]$$

$$ = {{ - 1} \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\,.\,{q^2}\left[ {{1 \over {{5 \over 4}r}} - {1 \over {{5 \over 3}r}}} \right]$$

$$ = {{ - 1} \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\,.\,{{{q^2}} \over {5r}}[4 - 3] = - {1 \over 5}\,.\,{1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\,.\,{{{q^2}} \over r}$$

content: Consider a region in free space bounded by the surfaces of an imaginary cube having sides of length a as shown in the figure. A charge + Q is placed at the centre O of the cube. P is such a point outside the cube that the line OP perpendicularly intersects the surface ABCD at R and also OR = RP = a/2. A charge + Q is placed at point P also. What is the total electric flux through the five faces of the cube other than ABCD?
explanation:
For surface ABCD electric flux is zero. Because at surface ABCD net electric field is zero. Using Gauss's law,

$$\oint {E.dS = } {{{Q_{in}}} \over {{\varepsilon _0}}}$$

Electric flux through the five faces of the cube, $${\phi _E} = {Q \over {{\varepsilon _0}}}$$

content: Four equal charges of value + Q are placed at any four vertices of a regular hexagon of side 'a'. By suitably choosing the vertices, what can be maximum possible magnitude of electric field at the centre of the hexagon?
explanation:
In $$\Delta AOM$$, $$\sin 30^\circ = {{AM} \over {AO}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$AO = {{{a \over 2}} \over {1/2}} = a$$

For maximum electric field at centre O charges should be placed at F, A, B and C.

$$ \because $$ Electric field due to charges at F and C is is equal and opposite at O.

$$ \therefore $$ Net electric field at centre O due to charges at A and B.

Angle between EA and EB is 60$$^\circ$$.

$$ \therefore $$ Enet at O,

$${E_{net}} = \sqrt {E_A^2 + E_B^2 + 2{E_A}{E_B}\cos 60^\circ } $$ ($$ \because $$ $${E_A} = {E_B} = E$$)

$$ = \sqrt {{E^2} + {E^2} + 2{E^2}\,.\,{1 \over 2}} $$ $$ \because $$ $$\left( {\cos 60^\circ = {1 \over 2}} \right)$$

$$E\sqrt 3 = {1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\,.\,{Q \over {{a^2}}}\sqrt 3 $$

content: The bob of a pendulum of mass m, suspended by an inextensible string of length L as shown in the figure carries a small charge q. An infinite horizontal plane conductor with uniform surface charge density $$\sigma$$ is placed below it. What will be the time period of the pendulum for small amplitude oscillations?
explanation:
$$ \therefore $$ Apparent weight of the bob, w' = mg $$-$$ qE

mg' = mg $$-$$ qE

$$ \therefore $$ g' = g $$-$$ $${{qE} \over m}$$

$$ \because $$ Time period of a pendulum,

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt {{L \over {{g_{eff}}}}} $$

$$ = 2\pi \sqrt {{L \over {g - {{qE} \over m}}}} = 2\pi \sqrt {{L \over {\left( {g - {{q\sigma } \over {{\varepsilon _0}m}}} \right)}}} $$

content: A positive charge Q is situated at the centre of a cube. The electric flux through any face of the cube is (in SI units)
explanation:
The positive charge Q is situated at the centre of a cube


According to Gauss theorem, the flux from any closed surface

$${\phi _E} = {Q \over {{\varepsilon _0}}}$$

Cube has 6 faces, so electric flux from any face $${\phi _E} = {Q \over {6{\varepsilon _0}}}$$

content: A charge of 0.8 coulomb is divided into two charges Q<sub>1</sub> and Q<sub>2</sub>. These are kept at a separation of 30 cm. The force on Q<sub>1</sub> is maximum when
explanation:
Checking the given options one by one

(i) Q1 = Q2 = 0.4 C

The force on Q1 due to Q2

$$F = {k_p}{{{Q_1}{ \times _2}} \over {30 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}$$


$$ = k{{{Q_1}{Q_2} \times 100} \over {30}}$$

$$ = k{{0.4 \times 0.4 \times 100} \over {30}}$$

$$ = {{k \times 0.16 \times 100} \over {30}}$$

(ii) When $${Q_1} = 0.8C\,{Q_2} \approx 0$$

$$F = {{k \times {Q_1}{Q_2}} \over {30 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}} \approx 0$$

(iii) When $${Q_1} \approx 0,{Q_2} = 0.8C$$

$$F = 0$$

(iv) $${Q_1} = 0.2C\,{Q_2} = 0.6C$$

$$F = {{k\,.\,0\,.\,2 \times 0.6} \over {30 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}$$

$$ = {{k \times 0.12 \times {{10}^2}} \over {30}}$$

Thus we find that, in option (a), the force on Q1 will be maximum.

$$ \therefore $$ correct answer is $${Q_1} = {Q_2} = 0.4C$$

content: A particle with charge Q coulomb, tied at the end of an inextensible string of length R metre, revolves in a vertical plane. At the centre of the circular trajectory, there is a fixed charge of magnitude Q coulomb. The mass of the moving charge M is such that $$Mg = {{{Q^2}} \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{R^2}}}$$. If at the highest position of the particle, the tension of the string just vanishes, the horizontal velocity at the lowest point has to be
explanation:
According to the question, when tension in the string is zero.


As T = 0

$$Mg - {{K{Q^2}} \over {{R^2}}} = {{m{v^2}} \over R}$$ $$\left( {k = {1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}} \right)$$

$${{m{v^2}} \over R}$$ = centripetal force required to keep the body in a circular path

$$ \Rightarrow $$ v = 0 [$$ \because $$ $$Mg = {{k{Q^2}} \over {{R^2}}}$$]

So the required work done to keep charge in vertical motion

$$ \therefore $$ $${W_g} = \Delta KE$$ = change in K.E.

$$ \Rightarrow mg(2R) = {1 \over 2}mv_0^2$$

$$v_0^2 = 2g\,.\,2R = 4gR$$

$${v_0} = \sqrt {4gR} = 2\sqrt {gR} $$

content: A unit negative charge with mass M resides at the mid-point of the straight line of length 2a adjoining two fixed charges of magnitude +Q each. If it is given a very small displacement x(x << a) in a direction perpendicular to the straight line, it will

options.content: come back to its original position and stay there

explanation:
From figure the net force

$${F_{net}} = - F\cos \theta + ( - F\cos \theta )$$

$$ = - 2F\cos \theta $$

$$ = - 2 \times {{kQ \times 1} \over {({x^2} + {a^2})}} \times {x \over {\sqrt {{x^2} + {a^2}} }}$$

$$ = - {{2kQ} \over {{{({x^2} + {a^2})}^{3/2}}}}.\,x$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${F_{net}} = - \left( {{{2kQ} \over {{a^3}}}} \right).\,x$$

Frequency of oscillation,

$$ = {1 \over {2\pi }}\sqrt {{{2kQ} \over {M{a^3}}}} $$

$$ = {1 \over {2\pi }}\sqrt {{{2 \times {1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}.\,Q} \over {M{a^3}}}} $$

$$ = {1 \over {2\pi }}\sqrt {{Q \over {2\pi {\varepsilon _0}M{a^3}}}} $$

content: A hollow metal sphere of radius R is charged with a charge Q. The electric potential and intensity inside the sphere are respectively
explanation:
Inside a hollow charged sphere, the electric field intensity is always zero.

$$ \therefore $$ E = 0

but potential is constant.



Potential on the surface of the hollow sphere

$$V = {{KQ} \over R} = {1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}.{Q \over R} = {Q \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}R}}$$

This is the potential (V) inside the charged hollow metal sphere.

content: Angle between an equipotential surface and electric lines of force is
explanation:
The electric lines of force are always perpendicular to the equipotential surface

$$\theta$$ = 90$$^\circ$$

content:
Consider the integral form of the Gauss' law in electrostatics

$$\oint {\overrightarrow E .d\overrightarrow S } = {Q \over {{\varepsilon _0}}}$$

Which of the following statements are correct?

options.content: An elementary patch on the enclosing surface is a polar vector.
An elementary patch on the enclosing surface is a pseudo-vector.

explanation:
To answer this question, we need to understand the various components of Gauss' Law in electrostatics and how it relates to the given options:

Gauss' Law in electrostatics is given by the integral form:

$$\oint {\overrightarrow E .d\overrightarrow S } = {Q \over {{\varepsilon _0}}}$$

In this equation:

  • $$\overrightarrow E$$ is the electric field vector.
  • $$d\overrightarrow S$$ is the differential vector element of the surface area on a closed surface.
  • $$Q$$ is the total charge enclosed within the surface.
  • $$\varepsilon_0$$ is the permittivity of free space.

Let's examine each option to determine which statements are correct:

Option A: It contains the law of Coulomb.

Gauss' Law is directly derived from Coulomb's law for a point charge. It states that the net electric flux through any closed surface is proportional to the charge enclosed within that surface. Therefore, this statement is correct. Gauss' Law essentially generalizes Coulomb's law to any closed surface.

Option B: It contains the superposition principle.

Gauss' Law inherently relies on the superposition principle. The total electric field due to multiple charges is the vector sum of the electric fields due to individual charges. This is because the law of superposition applies to electric fields, which are linear, allowing Gauss' Law to hold for systems with multiple charges. Hence, this statement is also correct.

Option C: An elementary patch on the enclosing surface is a polar vector.

The vector $$d\overrightarrow{S}$$ represents an infinitesimal area element on the surface, with the direction of the vector normal (perpendicular) to the surface. A normal vector to a surface is considered a polar vector because its properties do not change under a coordinate inversion. Therefore, this statement is correct.

Option D: An elementary patch on the enclosing surface is a pseudo-vector.

A pseudo-vector (or axial vector) is a vector that changes sign under inversion of the coordinate system (like angular momentum or magnetic field). The normal vector to a surface element does not change sign under inversion; hence, it is not a pseudo-vector but a polar vector. Thus, this statement is incorrect.

Therefore the correct statements are: Option A, Option B, and Option C.

content: A hemisphere of radius R is placed in a uniform electric field E so that its axis is parallel to the field. Which of the following statement(s) is/are true?

options.content: Flux through the curved surface of hemisphere is $$\pi {R^2}E$$.
Flux through the circular surface of hemisphere is $$\pi {R^2}E$$.
Work done in moving a point charge q from A to B via the path ACB depends upon R.

explanation:
According to Gauss's theorem

Net flux through the surface $$ = {{{\mathrm{q_{enclosed}}}} \over {{\varepsilon _0}}}$$

Here, $${\mathrm{q_{enclosed}}} = 0$$

So, $${\phi _{\mathrm{curved\,surface}}} + {\phi _{\mathrm{circular\,surface}}} = 0$$

$${\phi _{\mathrm{curved\,surface}}} = - \,{\phi _{\mathrm{circular\,surface}}}$$

$$ = - E\,.\,S = - ES\cos 180^\circ $$

$$ = ES$$ ($$\because$$ $$\cos 180^\circ = - 1$$)

$$ = E\,.\,\pi {R^2} = \pi {R^2}E$$

As, electrostatic field is conservative in nature.

So, work done is path independent.

So, option (a) and (c) are correct.

content: Two positive charges Q and 4Q are placed at points A and B respectively, where B is at a distance d units to the right of A. The total electric potential due to these charges is minimum at P on the line through A and B. What is (are) the distance (s) of P from A?

options.content: $${d \over 3}$$ units to the right of A
$${d \over 3}$$ units to the left of A
$${d \over 5}$$ units to the right of A
d units to the left of A

explanation:
$$ \because $$ VP is minimum.

$$ \therefore $$ EP = 0 $$ \Rightarrow $$ EA = EB

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}.{Q \over {{r^2}}} = {1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}.{{4Q} \over {{{(d - r)}^2}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over r} = {2 \over {d - r}} \Rightarrow d - r = 2r$$

$$ \therefore $$ Distance of P from the $$Ar = {d \over 3}$$ units to the right of A.

content: A charged particle of mass m<sub>1</sub> and charge q<sub>1</sub> is revolving in a circle of radius r. Another charged particle of charge q<sub>2</sub> and mass m<sub>2</sub> is situated at the centre of the circle. If the velocity and time period of the revolving particle be v and T respectively, then
explanation:
The necessary centripetal force, to move in a circular path is provided by the Coulomb's force.

$${{{m_1}{v^2}} \over r} = {1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}.{{{q_1}{q_2}} \over {{r^2}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt {{1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}.{{{q_1}{q_2}} \over {r{m_1}}}} $$ .... (i)

We know, $$v = r\omega $$

$$\omega = {{2\pi } \over T}$$

$$T = {{2\pi } \over \omega }$$

$$ = {{2\pi .\,r} \over v}$$ .... (ii)

Substitution the value of v from, Eq. (i) we get

$$T = {{2\pi r} \over {\sqrt {{{{q_1}{q_2}} \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}r.{m_1}}}} }}$$

$$T = \sqrt {{{4{\pi ^2}{r^2} \times 4\pi {\varepsilon _0}.r.{m_1}} \over {{q_1}{q_2}}}} $$

$$T = \sqrt {{{16{\pi ^3}{\varepsilon _0}{r^3}.\,{m_1}} \over {{q_1}{q_2}}}} $$

Both the charges are positive or both negative. Since, answers have the product q1q2 inside square root. Therefore, circular motion is not possible.

The statement of question is wrong. Either q1 or q2 should be negative.
